“Iran as Mithra’s Abode The first revolutionary thesis of this study is that Zoroastrianism can in no way, shape, or form, be considered an uncontested ideological foundation of Iranian Civilization, nor should Zarathustra be seen as the founder of this civilization in anything like the singular sense in which many Chinese would confer upon Confucius the honor of being the father of Chinese Civilization. Rather, if a single religious worldview defined Iranian identity in its formative phase, it was Mithraism, not Zoroastrianism. In Chapter 1, it is argued that Cyrus the Great was not a Zoroastrian, but a worshiper of Mithra and Anahita. Despite the intra-Iranian tribal conflicts between them, the Medes, Persians, Scythians, and Sarmatians were all worshipers of Mithra, the martial god of contracts or covenants, and some form of goddess figure closely associated with him. This goddess figure, variously known as Anahita, Artemis, Satana, or as an arch Gorgon (such as Medusa), was revered as the mother, lover, and initiatrix of Mithra himself or of the Mithraic hero. The opening chapter of this study also discusses how this form of spirituality, in which terrifying sacrifices, ritual intoxication, and the the ordeal by fire, the sacred[…]” Excerpt From Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode Jason Reza Jorjani This material may be protected by copyright.

column4: Summary
Mitra (Mithra) invoked in treaties as god of oaths and contracts, showing early Indo-Iranian religious influence.
Mithraism emerges as a dominant religious worldview among Iranian tribes, featuring worship of Mithra (god of contracts/covenants) and an associated goddess (Anahita, Artemis, Satana, or Gorgon).
Cyrus the Great argued to have been a devotee of Mithra and Anahita, not a Zoroastrian; Mithraism central to Iranian identity in this formative phase.
Mithra referenced in royal inscriptions; triad of Ahura Mazda, Anahita, and Mithra worshipped officially.
Roman Mithraism (Mithraic Mysteries) flourishes; cult spreads widely among soldiers, featuring secret rites, grades of initiation, and bull-slaying iconography.
Mithra remains significant in state religion alongside Anahita; Zoroastrianism becomes more dominant but does not fully suppress Mithraic traditions.
Muslim conquest leads to suppression of Mithra worship; traditions survive among Zoroastrian Parsees who flee to India.

column4: Summary
Mithra invoked as god of oath in peace treaty between Hittites and Mitanni, earliest textual evidence of Mithra.
Royal seal possibly depicts Mithra in tauroctony (bull-slaying) pose, showing early iconography.
Mithra as god of contract and mutual obligation, invoked in treaties.
Aryan migrations spread Mithra worship and solar symbolism into Iranian plateau.
Mithra associated with justice, contracts, and kingship; goddess figure emerges as Anahita.
Rituals of sacrifice, ordeal by fire, and intoxication become central to Mithraic spirituality.
Mithra worship widespread among Iranian tribes, including Medes and Persians.
Scythians and Sarmatians adopt Mithraism, integrating it with their own martial traditions.
Anahita, Artemis, and Satana become associated as goddess figures with Mithra, revered as mother/lover/initiate.
Mithraic rituals include terrifying sacrifices and spiritual ordeals.
Mithra seen as mediator, sun god, and guarantor of justice and contracts.
Mithra’s cult establishes role in kingship and warrior loyalty.
Probable beginning of organized Mithra worship; Mithra becomes central to Iranian religious identity.
Medes continue Mithraic worship, integrating goddess figures into state rituals.
Mithraic traditions spread westward through contact with Babylonians and Assyrians.
Mithraism influences Babylonian theology, with Mithra assimilated to Shamash.
Mithraic solar disc inspires design of royal crowns.
Cyrus the Great, argued to be a devotee of Mithra and Anahita, founds the Achaemenid Empire.
Mithra referenced in royal inscriptions; worship of Mithra and Anahita central to imperial ideology.
Persian kings receive first fruits at Mehragan festival, sacred to Mithra.
Continuation of Mithraic rites in royal court.
Darius promotes Ahura Mazda but retains Mithraic elements in state religion.
Mithraic rituals and contracts remain important among nobility and military.
Xerxes supports traditional Iranian religious practices, including Mithraic rites.
Mithra’s name invoked in legal and royal contexts.
Anahita’s cult rises in prominence alongside Mithra.
Artaxerxes II officially promotes triad of Ahura Mazda, Anahita, and Mithra in inscriptions.
Mithraic symbolism appears in royal iconography and architecture.
Mithraic rites continue despite growing Zoroastrian influence.
Mithra worship persists in provinces and among military elites.
Fall of Achaemenid Empire; Mithraic traditions continue among local populations.
Mithraic elements absorbed into Hellenistic religious syncretism.
Parthians continue Mithraic worship, especially among warrior aristocracy.
Mithraic festivals and rites remain central to Parthian identity.
Mithraic imagery appears on Parthian coinage and monuments.
Mithraic cult spreads westward into Asia Minor and the Mediterranean.
Colossal statues of Mithra erected at Mount Nemrut, showing syncretism with Greek and Persian deities.
Pirates practice secret Mithraic rites; Plutarch records their worship.
Mithraic cult introduced to Rome by Cilician pirates.
Mithraic mysteries begin to spread among Roman legions.
Mithraic chapels (mithraea) established in major Roman cities.
Mithraic mysteries flourish as a popular cult among soldiers and officials.
Merchants and bureaucrats join Mithraic cult; women excluded.
Mithraism reaches height of popularity; underground temples widespread.
Mithraic iconography (bull-slaying, sun disc) becomes standardized.
Mithraic mysteries begin to decline as Christianity rises.
Christianity suppresses rival cults, including Mithraism.
Mithraic temples closed; cult survives in secret.
Mithra retains role in state religion alongside Anahita and Ahura Mazda.
Fall of Sassanian Empire; Muslim conquest leads to suppression of Mithraic worship.
Zoroastrian Parsees flee to India, preserving Mithraic and Zoroastrian traditions.

column4: Summary
Median clans begin to unify in the Zagros region, laying the groundwork for future statehood.
Medes migrate into Iran from Asia, settling in the Zagros mountains and interacting with local cultures.
Deioces unites Median tribes, establishing the first centralized Median leadership and expelling Assyrians from northeastern Iran.
Median state consolidates power; begins to rival neighboring Assyria.
Phraortes expands Median control, further unifying the tribes and challenging Assyrian dominance.
Scythians invade Media, toppling Phraortes and establishing Scythian hegemony over the Medes for nearly three decades.
Scythian rule over Upper Asia: Madyes imposes Scythian dominance, with Hyrcania on the Median-Scythian frontier. This period is marked by conflict and cultural exchange.
Scythians expand their territory from the Halys River to the Caspian Sea, reaching the borders of Media and dominating West Asia.
Scythians and Cimmerians sack Sardis, capital of Lydia, destabilizing Anatolia and weakening Assyrian influence.
Scythians, with Lydian allies, defeat the Cimmerians and Treres, consolidating power in Anatolia and Media.
Scythian power peaks, ruling vast territories and influencing Median society and warfare.
Cyaxares leads Medes in overthrowing Scythian rule, restoring Median independence and beginning a new era of expansion.
Medes begin to expand into neighboring regions, forming alliances with Babylonians and Scythians to challenge Assyria.
Medes and Scythians ally to attack the Neo-Assyrian Empire, marking a shift from conflict to cooperation.
Medes, Scythians, and Babylonians sack Assur, weakening the Neo-Assyrian Empire.
The coalition captures Nineveh, ending Assyrian dominance in Mesopotamia and dividing its territories.
Final defeat of Neo-Assyrian remnants at Harran by the Medo-Babylonian-Scythian alliance.
Medes annex Urartu and Mannai, further expanding their empire and assimilating remaining Scythians.
Scythians retreat northward into the Ciscaucasian Steppe due to Median expansion; some remain and are assimilated into Median society.
Astyages ascends the Median throne, continuing policies of expansion and consolidation.
Conflict between Media and Lydia, partly due to Scythian groups fleeing into Lydian territory.
Cyrus the Great of Persia defeats Astyages, ending Median rule and beginning the Achaemenid Empire.
Cyrus captures Ecbatana, Median capital, consolidating Persian power and absorbing Median elites.
Persian Empire expands rapidly, incorporating former Median and Scythian territories.
Persian administration continues integration of Median and Scythian elements into imperial structure.
Darius I consolidates Persian rule, claims authority over Saka (Scythians) beyond the Black Sea, and launches campaigns into Scythian lands.
Darius I invades Scythian territory with a massive army; outcome unclear, but Scythians maintain independence and gain reputation for invincibility among Greeks.
Iranian colonization of Ionia influences rise of Pre-Socratic Greek philosophy; philosophical exchanges between Iranians and Greeks intensify.
Persian attempts to subdue Greek city-states, leading to famous battles and the eventual rise of Greek identity in opposition to Persia.
Xerxes I leads massive Persian invasion of Greece; Greek resistance shapes Western historical narrative.
Pre-Socratic philosophers such as Heraclitus and Pythagoras are influenced by Iranian thought; Pythagoras studies in Persian capital, Heraclitus interacts with Darius I.
Peace established between Persia and Athens, ending Persian claims to Greek colonies and stabilizing the region.
Scythian influence wanes as Sarmatians and other tribes rise in the Pontic Steppe.
Sarmatians begin to push Scythians out of their traditional territories, leading to further decline.
Sarmatians, especially the Roxolani, destroy Scythian capital at Kamianka, ending Scythian dominance and dispersing their culture.
Sarmatians reach Chersonesus, destroying Greek and native settlements along the Black Sea.
Sarmatians destroy most settlements on the north shore of the Black Sea, completing their dominance over former Scythian lands.
Continued destruction of settlements by Sarmatians; Scythian remnants assimilated or displaced.
Sarmatians solidify control over Pontic Steppe, replacing Scythians as dominant force.
Sarmatian pressure halts further southward movement by the Bastarnae, stabilizing new tribal boundaries in the region.
Scythian material culture disappears; forest steppe peoples regain independence and sedentary lifestyles.
Sarmatia Europa becomes the new name for the Pontic Steppe, marking the end of Scythian era.
Greek inscriptions cease mentioning Scythian slaves, reflecting the collapse of Scythian power and trade.
Forest steppe tribes north of the Black Sea become independent, free from Scythian or Sarmatian control.
Bastarnae halt Sarmatian expansion along the Istros river, establishing new power dynamics in the region.
Greek colonies on the Black Sea suffer decline due to ongoing tribal invasions and shifting trade routes.
Scythian identity and culture effectively disappear from historical records, replaced by Sarmatian and other tribal identities.

Zarathustra’s historical context is the Median-Scythian war; Hyrcania is a frontier zone. Zarathustra is recast as the first philosopher, his thought emerging from the Indo-European psycho-social milieu, not isolated from Greek and Indian philosophy.

Zarathustra’s teachings are seen as the beginning of a philosophical tradition that influences later Greek thinkers, especially in the context of Iranian colonization of Ionia and the rise of Pre-Socratic philosophy.

Zarathustra is positioned as the first voice in a philosophical dialogue that includes Pythagoras (who studied in Persia) and Heraclitus (summoned by Darius I), highlighting the Iranian roots of philosophical inquiry.
Pythagoras studies in Persian capital for 12 years; Heraclitus engages with the Persian court, showing deep ties between Iranian and Greek intellectual traditions.
Zarathustra’s fallibility is emphasized, contrasting him with the mythic status of Confucius in China; he is seen as a human philosopher within a wider Indo-European tradition.

column1: “By writing in an archaic language almost identical to the oldest form of Sanskrit, Zarathustra was attempting to imbue his message with the authority of a language that he knew to be ancestral to both his own branch of the Iranian community and to that which he was confronting. What scholars have arbitrarily named “Gathic Avestan” may simply be poor Rig Vedic Sanskrit, the way that French had its origins in improper Latin and bears traces of other languages that had been spoken in Roman Gaul. That is as much as to say that the reason why “Gathic” Avestan is a linguistic isolate, is that Zarathustra, who probably spoke “Young Avestan,” was trying to compose in the oldest and most theologically authoritative form of Sanskrit — a language with such authority that it was called devâ-nagiri or the “language of the gods.” If Zarathustra was going to preach against the devâs (divs), what better tongue to do that in than their own? ” Excerpt From Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode Jason Reza Jorjani This material may be protected by copyright.

Zarathustra’s use of an archaic language nearly identical to the oldest form of Sanskrit-what scholars call “Gathic Avestan”-has been widely noted for its remarkable similarity to Rig Vedic Sanskrit134578. This linguistic closeness is so pronounced that, as noted by scholars like T. Burrow and in The Cambridge History of India, many lines from the Gathas can be rendered into Sanskrit with only systematic phonetic substitutions58. The two languages share not just grammar and vocabulary, but also religious terminology and mythic references, such as “asura/ahura” and “deva/daeva,” though with reversed theological roles358.

While some scholars interpret this similarity as evidence that Zarathustra lived in the same era as the Rig Vedic rishis, others-echoing the view in your excerpt-argue that Zarathustra may have been intentionally composing in an archaic, authoritative register to lend weight to his message15. Just as French evolved from Latin but retained Latin for formal and religious purposes, it is possible that Zarathustra, who likely spoke a more contemporary form of Iranian (“Young Avestan”), chose to write in the older, sacred language associated with the ancestral Indo-Iranian tradition158. This would have allowed him to address his audience-and critique the deva-worshipping tradition-in the “language of the gods” (devâ-nagiri), thereby maximizing the theological authority and resonance of his teachings15.

In summary, while Gathic Avestan is a linguistic isolate today, its archaic features may reflect a deliberate attempt by Zarathustra to invoke the prestige and authority of the primordial Indo-Iranian religious tradition, rather than providing a definitive chronological marker for his lifetime158.

column4: Basis/Explanation

Zarathustra composed in an archaic language (“Gathic Avestan”) nearly identical to the oldest form of Sanskrit (Rig Vedic), likely to imbue his message with theological authority by using the “language of the gods” (devâ-nagiri).
column5: “Gathic Avestan” may be a deliberate archaic register rather than a spoken language; Zarathustra probably spoke “Younger Avestan.”

column4: The similarity between Gathic Avestan and Rig Vedic Sanskrit reflects Zarathustra’s intent to preach against the devâs (divs) in their own sacred tongue, enhancing the authority of his critique.
column5: This parallels how classical languages (e.g., Latin, Sanskrit) are used for religious texts long after everyday speech changes.

column1: Zarathustra’s use of archaic Gathic Avestan was likely a conscious choice to connect with the ancestral Indo-Iranian religious tradition, using a prestigious “language of the gods.”
This explains why Gathic Avestan is a linguistic isolate and not necessarily evidence of Zarathustra’s exact historical era.

Period of Indo-Iranian migrations and split between proto-Iranians and proto-Indo-Aryans.
column5: Relevant for context of Zoroaster’s teachings.27

column4: Some scholars prefer a slightly later date for Zarathustra based on social and linguistic features.
Zarathustra’s reforms challenge animal sacrifice and ritual intoxication among Iranians.
column5: Zoroaster’s critique of Bronze Age Iranian religion.7

column4: Attempts to link Zarathustra historically to the Achaemenid era and related events.
column5: Some historians accept a date just before or contemporary with Cyrus the Great.1238

column4: “258 years before Alexander” (interpreted as before Alexander’s conquest of Persia in 330 BCE), placing Zarathustra’s birth at 628 BCE.
column5: Tradition cited by Al-Biruni and others; places Zarathustra in the Median period.1268

column4: Reign of Cyrus the Great, founder of the Achaemenid Empire.
column5: Zoroastrianism present but not yet state religion.123

column4: Zoroastrianism is adopted by the Achaemenid Empire as a major religious tradition.
column5: Darius the Great (r. 522–486 BCE) is a strong proponent.367

column4: Herodotus and other Greeks describe Persian religious practices, noting differences from Greek religion.
column5: No direct mention of Zoroaster, but Zoroastrian influence apparent.8

column4: Alexander the Great conquers Persia, ending the Achaemenid Empire.
column5: Beginning of Hellenistic influence in Iran.123

column4: Zoroastrianism continues as a major religion under the Parthian Empire.
Zoroastrianism becomes the official state religion of Persia under the Sassanian Empire.
column5: State sponsorship leads to codification and expansion.1367

column4: Zoroastrian religious literature committed to writing under Shapur II.
column5: Avesta and other texts are compiled and preserved.3

column4: Zoroastrian religious works further written and codified.
Muslim Arab conquest leads to suppression of Zoroastrianism and forced conversions.
Zoroastrianism persists in Iran, India (Parsis), and globally, despite centuries of decline.
column5: Modern revival and scholarly study continue.27

column1: do the same for this content: “By writing in an archaic language almost identical to the oldest form of Sanskrit, Zarathustra was attempting to imbue his message with the authority of a language that he knew to be ancestral to both his own branch of the Iranian community and to that which he was confronting. What scholars have arbitrarily named “Gathic Avestan” may simply be poor Rig Vedic Sanskrit, the way that French had its origins in improper Latin and bears traces of other languages that had been spoken in Roman Gaul. That is as much as to say that the reason why “Gathic” Avestan is a linguistic isolate, is that Zarathustra, who probably spoke “Young Avestan,” was trying to compose in the oldest and most theologically authoritative form of Sanskrit — a language with such authority that it was called devâ-nagiri or the “language of the gods.” If Zarathustra was going to preach against the devâs (divs), what better tongue to do that in than their own? ” Excerpt From Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode Jason Reza Jorjani This material may be protected by copyright.

column4: Basis/Explanation

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The language of the Gathas (“Gathic Avestan”) is nearly identical to Rig Vedic Sanskrit; many words and verses can be directly translated between the two with systematic changes.
column5: The two languages share roots in Proto-Indo-Iranian; the split was recent at the time of composition.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Zarathustra may have intentionally used an archaic, Sanskrit-like language to lend his teachings the authority of the ancestral “language of the gods” (devâ-nagiri), even though he likely spoke a more contemporary dialect (“Younger Avestan”).
column5: Similar to how Latin was used in medieval Europe for religious authority even after it was no longer spoken colloquially.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Scholars note that “Gathic Avestan” is a linguistic isolate, possibly because it was an intentional archaizing register rather than a naturally evolving spoken language.
column5: The Gathas are unique; other Zoroastrian scriptures are in “Younger Avestan.”

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Both the Gathas and the Rig Veda reference similar deities (e.g., Ahura/Asura, Daeva/Deva) but with reversed theological roles; this reflects a shared Indo-Iranian heritage and a period of religious divergence.
column5: Theological inversion is a key marker of the Iranian-Hindu split.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Some verses in the Gathas and the Vedas are nearly identical in content and meter, further supporting the close relationship between the two traditions.
column5: Zhand Avesta and Rig Veda share themes, structure, and poetic forms.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Zarathustra’s choice to compose in the ancestral language allowed him to critique the devas (divs) in their own sacred tongue, maximizing the impact and authority of his message among both Iranians and their Vedic kin.
column5: This strategy mirrors other religious reformers who use prestigious archaic registers for authority.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The close similarity between Gathic Avestan and Rig Vedic Sanskrit indicates that the Indo-Iranian languages had only recently diverged at the time these texts were composed.
column5: The Cambridge History of India and scholars like T. Burrow have emphasized this point.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The Gathas’ language, characters, and religious concepts reflect a shared Indo-Iranian world that included both the ancestors of Iranians and Indo-Aryans (Vedic peoples).
column5: Many principal names and terms are recognizable in both traditions.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The Gathas and the Rig Veda both use similar poetic meters and hymnic structures, suggesting a common ritual and literary heritage.
column5: Both traditions have collections called “Gathas” and use similar syllabic meters (e.g., Gayatri, Ushnih, Pankti).

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Most scholars agree that the Gathas’ archaic language is not simply a fossil but a deliberate choice, paralleling the use of classical languages in later religious traditions.
column5: This complicates attempts to date Zarathustra solely on linguistic grounds.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Direct word-for-word translation is possible between Old Avestan and Rig Vedic Sanskrit by reversing sound laws and stylistic elements.
column5: Reichelt and others have demonstrated this linguistic closeness.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Zarathustra’s reforms included a sharp dichotomy between good (Ahura Mazda, asuras) and evil (daevas), inverting the Vedic hierarchy of gods and demons.
column5: This innovation is seen as a major turning point in Indo-Iranian religious history.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The preservation of the Gathas in an archaic form may reflect a strong oral tradition that maintained older linguistic features for ritual and theological reasons.
column5: Oral transmission can preserve archaic forms long after they fall out of everyday use.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The period marks the final phase before the full cultural and religious separation of the Indo-Aryan (Vedic) and Iranian (Avestan) peoples.
column5: Reflected in both language and mythological content.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Zarathustra’s use of the ancestral language reinforced a distinct Iranian religious identity in opposition to the Vedic tradition.
column5: This helped establish Zoroastrianism as a separate faith.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Later Zoroastrian and Hindu scriptures continued to reference and adapt themes, verses, and structures from these archaic sources.
column5: The influence persists in the Yashts, Khorda-Avesta, and Hindu Gathas.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Scholars often study the Gathas and Rig Veda together, as they illuminate each other’s meanings, context, and development.
column5: “No scholar of the Avesta worth the designation can do without a thorough grounding in Vedic Sanskrit.” (HD Griswold)

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Many ritual practices, such as fire worship and invocation of similar deities, continued in both Zoroastrian and Vedic traditions long after their initial divergence.
column5: Shows the persistence of shared Indo-Iranian religious culture.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Some verses and prayers in the Avesta are direct translations of Vedic verses, differing only in dialect and minor stylistic features.
column5: This supports the theory of a shared liturgical tradition.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: The poetic forms of the Gathas (e.g., Gayatri, Ushnih, Pankti) mirror those of the Rig Veda, indicating a common metrical heritage.
column5: This similarity extends to the structure of prayers and hymns in both traditions.

column2: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age
column4: Zarathustra’s polemic against the devas (divs) in their own sacred language was a strategic move to challenge the authority of rival priests and traditions.
column5: Reinforced the legitimacy and reach of his religious reforms.

column1: do the same table style for this content. only include rows relevant to this information: “The third date proposed for Zarathustra has only really been argued for by a single scholar, Mary Settegast, with all advocates of this date deferring entirely to her work. Settegast introduces this thesis in her book Plato Prehistorian, which is an archeological evaluation of the historical reality of Plato’s legend of Atlantis.43 She then expands upon the thesis of an 8,500 year old Zarathustra in a book titled When Zarathustra Spoke, which is devoted solely to making that argument.44 Settegast notes how several classical Greek authors make the claim that Zarathustra lived circa 6,500 BC. The Lydian historian Xanthus tells us that Zarathustra lived 6,000 years before Xerxes crossed the Hellespont for the conquest of Greece.45 We also have Eudoxus of Cnidus (404–355 BC) telling us that Zarathustra lived 6,000 years before the death of Plato.46 Supposedly, this is something that the Magi who attended Plato’s funeral mentioned at that ceremony. Aristotle agrees with that date, and then Plutarch (45–120 CE) tells us that Zarathustra’s life transpired some 5,000 years before the Trojan War.47 All of this points to the late Neolithic, about 6,500 BC or 8,500 years before the present time. ” Excerpt From Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode Jason Reza Jorjani This material may be protected by copyright.

column4: Basis/Explanation

Settegast, in When Zarathustra Spoke, argues Zarathustra lived around 6500 BCE, based on archaeological interpretation and ancient Greek sources claiming Zarathustra lived 6,000 years before Xerxes or Plato.
column5: This thesis is unique to Settegast and not accepted by mainstream scholarship156.

column4: Greek authors such as Xanthus, Eudoxus, Aristotle, and Plutarch claimed Zarathustra lived 5,000–6,000 years before the Trojan War or Plato’s death, placing him in the late Neolithic.
column5: These claims are cited by Settegast but are generally considered legendary by historians.

column4: Settegast links her thesis to broader archaeological interpretations of Neolithic religion and culture, drawing parallels to Plato’s legend of Atlantis and the spread of agriculture and religious reform in the Neolithic Near East.
column5: Presented in When Zarathustra Spoke and Plato Prehistorian, but not widely adopted167.

column4: Basis/Explanation

Settegast argues Zarathustra lived 8,500 years ago (ca. 6500–6200 BCE), based on archaeological interpretations and ancient Greek sources that date Zarathustra 6,000 years before Xerxes or Plato12345.
column5: This thesis is unique to Settegast and not accepted by mainstream scholarship.

column4: Ancient authors (Xanthus, Eudoxus, Plutarch, Aristotle) claimed Zarathustra lived 5,000–6,000 years before the Trojan War or Plato’s death, placing him in the late Neolithic134.
column5: These claims are cited by Settegast but are generally considered legendary by historians.

column4: Settegast links this dating to evidence of transformative cultural and religious change in the Neolithic Near East, suggesting Zarathustra’s teachings coincided with the spread of agriculture and new social patterns234.
column5: Presented in When Zarathustra Spoke and Plato Prehistorian; not widely adopted in academic circles.

column1: do the same with this content: “Settegast attempts to marshal archeological evidence from transformations in late Neolithic society as evidence for this astoundingly early date by interpreting these remains as corroborative of certain themes in the Gathas. In the Gathas we see that Zarathustra is preaching against a ritualistic, sacrificial religion and against warlords who are allied with the priests of that religion. They would carry out cattle raids and sacrifice cattle that had been captured in those raids. Dwellings of the late Neolithic period in the area of Western Anatolia, an area which later became Kurdistan, are full of bulls horns. Bulls horns are on walls and benches, while there is hardly any trace of agriculture. There are a lot of implements of war and of hunting. All of a sudden, around the date that classical authors give us for Zarathustra, there appears to be a transition to an agricultural society.48 The bulls horns disappear. The warlike implements disappear. There is the rise of a pottery tradition that involves new firing techniques that produced patterns with a high contrast between light and dark.49 Settegast takes this to be significant because both fire and light/dark duality are at the core of Zarathustra’s[…]” Excerpt From Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode Jason Reza Jorjani This material may be protected by copyright.

column4: Basis/Explanation

Settegast uses archaeological evidence from Western Anatolia and Kurdistan to argue for a Zarathustra around 6500–6200 BCE. She interprets the disappearance of bull horns, war implements, and the rise of agricultural and pottery traditions as reflecting themes in the Gathas, such as opposition to cattle raiding, sacrificial rituals, and warlords allied with priests1234578.
column5: This thesis is unique to Settegast and not accepted by mainstream scholarship.

column4: Ancient Greek authors (Xanthus, Pliny, Eudoxus, Plutarch) dated Zarathustra to 6500–6200 BCE, claiming he lived 5,000–6,000 years before the Trojan War or Plato’s death, aligning with the late Neolithic15.
column5: These claims are cited by Settegast but are generally considered legendary by historians.

column4: Settegast links the archaeological transition from a warlike, cattle-raiding society to an agricultural one with the reformist themes in the Gathas, such as criticism of ritual sacrifice and the importance of fire and light/dark duality, which she sees reflected in new pottery styles23478.
column5: Presented in When Zarathustra Spoke and Plato Prehistorian; not widely adopted in academic circles.

column4: Basis/Explanation

Settegast interprets archaeological evidence from Western Anatolia/Kurdistan-such as dwellings filled with bulls’ horns, war and hunting implements, and little agriculture-as reflecting a ritualistic, sacrificial, cattle-raiding society, similar to the one Zarathustra critiques in the Gathas. Around 6500–6200 BCE, there is a transition: bulls’ horns and war implements disappear, agriculture rises, and new pottery with strong light/dark patterns appears, which Settegast links to Zarathustra’s emphasis on fire and duality12345.
column5: This interpretation is unique to Settegast and not accepted by mainstream scholarship; it links Neolithic social transformation to Zoroastrian reform themes.

column4: The shift from a cattle-raiding, sacrificial culture to an agricultural and pottery-producing society is seen as archaeological support for the kind of religious and social reform described in the Gathas234.
column5: Settegast’s thesis challenges conventional datings of Zarathustra (e.g., 630 BCE, 1500–1200 BCE)45.
